children.value: , where the reverse-time SDE is the basis of score-based generation, and to
, where Langevin dynamics exploits stationary distributions of SDEs.

Stochastic differential equations (SDEs) describe the continuous-time evolution of systems driven by random noise. They are the continuous-time counterpart of the linear dynamical systems studied in Part IV and underpin a broad range of models in statistics, physics, finance, and machine learning. In this course they arise most prominently in two places: as the principled mathematical framework for denoising diffusion models, and as a lens through which many stationary Gaussian processes can be formulated and computed with efficiently.
The fundamental source of randomness in continuous-time stochastic systems is

children.value: , existence and uniqueness of a strong solution are guaranteed.

Why Gaussian noise? Beyond analytical tractability, the central limit theorem gives physical justification: a large number of small independent perturbations accumulate into something Gaussian. The SDE framework can be generalized to other driving processes (any semimartingale), but Gaussian noise is by far the most common choice.

Linear SDEs and the Ornstein–Uhlenbeck Process

children.label: Linear SDEs and the Ornstein–Uhlenbeck Process

children.value: The most tractable family of SDEs has a drift that is linear in the state:

children.html: dut=(A(t)ut+b(t))dt+G(t) dwt.d\mathbf{u}_t = \bigl(A(t)\mathbf{u}_t + \mathbf{b}(t)\bigr)dt + G(t)\,d\mathbf{w}_t.dut​=(A(t)ut​+b(t))dt+G(t)dwt​.

children.value: Because the noise enters linearly, solutions have Gaussian transition densities and therefore define Gaussian processes.

children.html: E(θ)=−log⁡p(x∣θ)−log⁡p(θ)E(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\log p(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) - \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta})E(θ)=−logp(x∣θ)−logp(θ)

children.value: , and Langevin dynamics provides a continuous-time limit of gradient-based MCMC. Discretization via Euler–Maruyama gives the unadjusted Langevin algorithm (ULA); adding a Metropolis–Hastings correction step recovers the Metropolis-adjusted Langevin algorithm (MALA).
Since solutions to linear SDEs have Gaussian finite-dimensional distributions, they are Gaussian processes. The GP mean and covariance can be computed from the SDE coefficients:

children.html: m(t)=E[ut],k(s,t)=Cov⁡(us,ut),m(t) = \mathbb{E}[u_t], \qquad k(s, t) = \operatorname{Cov}(u_s, u_t),m(t)=E[ut​],k(s,t)=Cov(us​,ut​),

children.value: both of which satisfy ODEs derivable from the SDE.
Conversely, many classical stationary covariance kernels correspond exactly to the stationary GPs generated by specific linear SDEs:

children.html: k(r)=(1+3ℓr)e−3ℓrk(r) = (1 + \sqrt{3}\ell r)e^{-\sqrt{3}\ell r}k(r)=(1+3​ℓr)e−3​ℓr
k(r)=(1+5ℓr+53ℓ2r2)e−5ℓrk(r) = (1 + \sqrt{5}\ell r + \tfrac{5}{3}\ell^2 r^2)e^{-\sqrt{5}\ell r}k(r)=(1+5​ℓr+35​ℓ2r2)e−5​ℓr

children.value: This result is the mathematical heart of
: design a forward SDE that converts data into noise, estimate the score

children.html: ∇ulog⁡pt\nabla_u \log p_t∇u​logpt​

children.value: using a neural network trained by

, then run the reverse SDE to generate new data. The connection between the reverse-time correction and the score function explains why learning to denoise is equivalent to learning to generate — a remarkable duality discussed further in the Denoising Diffusion Models chapter.
